population: Adult patients (age ≥18 years) with sepsis (defined by Sepsis-3 criteria) admitted to internal medicine or surgical ICUs.
predictor: Hematocrit (HCT) level (categorized as low, regular, or high based on sex-specific quartiles)
timing: HCT measured within the first 24 hours of ICU admission; mortality assessed at 30 days.
method: Retrospective cohort study using the MIMIC-IV database (2008–2019); univariate and multivariable Cox proportional hazards regression; Kaplan-Meier survival analysis.
effect_size: Low HCT vs. Regular HCT: Adjusted HR = 1.589 (95% CI: 1.099–2.297, p < 0.001); High HCT vs. Regular HCT: Adjusted HR = 1.330 (95% CI: 1.007–1.757, p = 0.055).
notes: Low HCT was identified as an independent risk factor for 30-day mortality. HCT levels were defined by sex: Low (male ≤42%, female ≤37%), Regular (male 42%–49%, female 37%–44%), and High (male ≥49%, female ≥44%). Evidence extracted from text, Tables 1-3, and Figure 1 (Kaplan-Meier curves). A discrepancy was noted between the abstract and Table 3 regarding the confidence interval; Table 3 values were used.
